segments.s025-g89: جهاندار ('sovereign') is the subject placed at the start for emphasis; پیش از تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before you' (پیش + از + تو), and بسیار بود is the predicate 'was many' (بسیار = 'many', بود = past 'was'), so the whole segment means 'there were many sovereigns before you.'
segments.s025-g90: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'); تخت مهی را is the definite object 'the moon-like throne' (مهی = 'moon-like'), را marks it as definite/object, and سزاوار بود is the predicative phrase 'was deserving/fit'; together: 'who were worthy of the moon-like throne.'
segments.s025-g91: فراوان qualifies 'غم و شادمانی' as 'many/abundant sorrows and joys'; شمرد is a past-tense transitive verb 'he counted/numbered' with its direct object 'غم و شادمانی' (word order: object phrase before verb is normal poetic variation).
segments.s025-g92: برفت is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'he went away / departed' (poetic past), و links to the next clause, جهان دیگری را is the definite object 'another world' (را marks the object), and سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/handed over' — 'he departed and handed the world to another.'
segments.s025-g93: اگر marks a conditional 'if'; باره آهنینی is a noun phrase 'an iron bāreh / an iron burden/load' (باره here = 'load/burden'), and به پای is a locative phrase 'at the foot of...' with پای = 'foot/base (of)'; this sets the protasis 'if an iron load at the foot of...'.
segments.s025-g94: سپهرت is 'your sky/heaven' (possession marked by -ت). بساید is a verbal form here meaning roughly 'should alight/settle' or 'come to rest' (poetic/archaic usage), نمانی is second-person present/subjunctive 'you would not remain' or 'you would not be left', and به جای 'in place' completes the clause: 'you would not remain in your place.'
segments.s025-g95: کسی را بود is an impersonal/anticipatory construction 'there would be someone' (کسی = 'someone', را marks topic/emphasis, بود = past/existential 'was/would be'), زین سپس is an adverbial/temporal phrase 'after this/thereafter' (سپس = 'then/afterward'); تخت تو is the object 'your throne' — together: 'there would be someone thereafter regarding your throne.'
segments.s025-g96: به خاک اندر آرد is a verb phrase 'brings/casts down to the earth' (به خاک = 'to the earth', اندر آرد = 'puts/throws into'), سر و بخت تو lists the things brought low 'your head and your fortune' (سر = 'head', بخت = 'fortune'), connected by و; word order places the goal 'به خاک' before the verb for emphasis as typical in poetic diction.
segments.s025-g41: This is a short clause with the colloquial/poetic subject pronoun 'تو' + the present-tense verb 'داری' (you have). Word order is subject–verb–object. 'جهان' is the direct object and 'زیر انگشتری' is a prepositional phrase modifying it: literally 'the world under a ring' (i.e., under your ring/authority).
segments.s025-g42: A simple enumerative noun phrase joined by the conjunction 'و' (and). The list names kinds of beings placed under the ring in the previous segment; the conjunctions are the normal coordinating 'and'.
segments.s025-g43: 'ز هر کشوری' is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from every country/land'. 'گرد کن' is an imperative construction: 'گرد' (here used as a verbal root meaning 'go about/collect, assemble') + the imperative 'کن' = 'gather/assemble'. 'مهتران' is the plural noun 'the great ones/chiefs' — the direct object of the imperative.
segments.s025-g44: 'از' again marks source/selection 'from'. The two nouns 'اخترشناسان و افسونگران' are the categories being summoned: 'astrologers' and 'enchanters/sorcerers', joined by the coordinating 'و'.
segments.s025-g45: 'سخن سربه‌سر موبدان را بگوی' is an imperative instruction. 'سخن' is 'words/speech'; 'سربه‌سر' here intensifies or specifies 'entirely/fully' (i.e., give all the words verbatim); 'موبدان را' is the definite object marked by 'را' ('the words of the mobeds'); 'بگوی' is an imperative form meaning 'say/tell'.
segments.s025-g46: Two coordinated imperatives. 'پژوهش کن' = 'do an investigation / investigate' (پژوهش + imperative کن). 'و' links the commands. 'راستی بازجوی' is another hortative: 'راستی' = 'the truth' and 'بازجوی' (here functioning as an imperative/verb) = 'inquire/search out' (seek the truth).
segments.s025-g47: 'نگه کن' is an imperative compound: 'نگه' (gaze/watch/take heed) + 'کن' (do) = 'look/attend'. 'که' introduces the content clause 'that/which' or 'who'. 'هوش تو' = 'your intellect/awareness' (possessive order: noun + pronoun). 'بر دست' is a literary phrase meaning 'in the power/hand of'; 'کیست' is the copula question 'who is (it)'. Overall: 'Take heed which hand has your intellect / who controls your mind.'
segments.s025-g48: This is a conditional/alternative: 'ز مردم شمار' = literally 'count/from the people count' = 'determine whether it is among people' — here 'شمار' acts as the notion of counting/considering. 'ار' is the old poetic 'if' (introducing the alternative). 'ز دیو و پریست' states the alternative source: 'or (if) it is of demons and fairies' (the final '-ست' is the copula 'is').
segments.s025-g25: هَمانِ جملهٔ اصلی است: زمین هفت کشور = 'the land/realm of seven countries' — a poetic inversion where the modifier هفت کشور follows زمین without an explicit ezāfe. به فرمانِ تو است is the predicate: به + noun (فرمان) indicates being 'under/at the command' of تو, and است is the copula in present tense.
segments.s025-g26: A coordinated nominal subject (دد و دام و مردم) followed by the same predicate pattern به پیمان تو است: the conjunctions و join 'enemy/hostility, trap, and people', and به پیمانِ تو = 'by/under your covenant'; است is the copula. The whole clause states these things are subject to the addressee's pact/authority.
segments.s025-g27: به خورشیدرویان is the indirect object 'to the sun-faced ones' (به + plural noun). جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' and گفت is the past verb 'said'; Persian often puts the verb before the subject in poetic prose, so the literal order here is 'to X the sovereign said'.
segments.s025-g28: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). چونین modifies شگفتی as 'such' (چونین شگفتی = 'such a marvel'), بشاید is a classical modal/optative meaning 'it would be fitting/it may be proper' and takes the following verbal/adjectival complement نهفت ('hidden/concealed'). Together: 'that it would be fitting that such a wonder be hidden.'
segments.s025-g29: که‌گر (archaic compound = اگر) introduces a conditional 'if'. از من این داستان بشنوید is 'hear this story from me' — بشنوید is 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive form 'you hear' or 'you be told', so grammatically this is the protasis 'if you hear this tale from me.'
segments.s025-g30: شودتان is an optative/impersonal construction meaning 'may it become to you' or 'may it befall you' with the clitic تان marking plural addressee; دل از جان من ناامید is the complement: 'your heart become hopeless from/regarding my life' — i.e. 'may your hearts despair about my life.'
segments.s025-g31: به شاه گرانمایه is the indirect object 'to the noble/esteemed king' (به + title + epithet). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said', and ارنواز following the verb is the subject (verb–subject order typical in classical Persian narrative): 'Ernawaz said to the esteemed king.'
segments.s025-g32: که introduces the content of the speech. بر ما here means 'to/for us' (direction/beneficiary), بباید is the modal 'must/ought', and گشادنت is the infinitive گشادن with the 2nd-person singular suffix -ت ('your revealing' / 'your opening'), so the clause literally says 'that your revealing of the secret must (be) to us' — i.e. 'you must reveal your secret to us.' راز is the object 'secret.'
segments.s025-g121: Subject phrase with epithet and place: گرانمایه (an epithet/noun 'the esteemed one') is the subject; از پیش تخت بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from before the lofty throne' where از + پیش indicate motion/source 'from in front of' and تخت بلند is 'lofty throne' (adjective follows noun). Word order is topical: subject then place-from phrase preparing for the verb.
segments.s025-g122: Predicate clause: بتابید is 3rd-person past of تابیدن used here in the sense 'to turn' (toward/away) or 'to turn/turn aside'; روی is the direct object 'face'; از نهیب گزند is a compound prepositional phrase 'from the rebuke's harm' (از = 'from', نهیب = 'rebuke/stern cry', گزند = 'harm'). Together: 'he turned his face away from the harm of the rebuke.'
segments.s025-g123: Temporal clause introduced by چو ('when'); آمد is simple past 'came' and دل نامور is the subject 'the renowned heart' (noun + epithet). باز جای 'back to its place' follows the verb as the complement indicating return — literal order: 'When the renowned heart came back to (its) place.'
segments.s025-g124: Main clause: به تخت کیان اندر آورد پای is idiomatic: به + تخت کیان = 'onto the throne of the Kians', اندر آورد (آورد past 'brought') + پای ('foot') = 'brought/placed (his) foot' = 'set foot (on)'. اندر here stresses 'into/on/within' the throne area. The verb آورد governs پای as the thing placed.
segments.s025-g125: نشان فریدون is a genitival phrase 'the sign/standard of Fereydun' (noun + proper name). به گرد جهان is a circumpositional phrase 'around the world': به (towards/into) + گرد ('around') + جهان ('world'), marking the space in which the action occurs.
segments.s025-g126: Verb and adverbial phrase: همی (a poetic particle) indicates continuous/habitual or emphatic aspect here ('once more/continually'); باز جست is past 'again leapt' (باز='again', جست = 'leapt/sprung'). آشکار و نهان are a paired predicate/adverbial 'apparent and hidden' (visible and concealed), describing how the sign manifested around the world.
segments.s025-g127: Negative enumeration: نه ... نه ... و نه expresses three coordinated negations. آرام بودش = 'he had no calm/peace' (بودش = past 'was' with enclitic third-person), followed by نه خواب ('nor sleep') and و نه خورد ('and nor eating/food') — the verb بودش in the first conjunct is understood to apply to the following items.
segments.s025-g128: Resultative descriptive clause: شده (perfect/past participial use of 'become') + روز روشن ('the bright day') is the subject phrase, بر او 'upon him' indicates location/experience, and لاژورد (lapis-lazuli/azure) is the predicate complement 'became azure'. Literal order is 'the bright day had become lapis-blue upon him.'
segments.s025-g33: توانیم کردن = توانستن + کردن: a capability construction meaning “we can do” (here plural, present/optative sense). مگر here functions as an adversative/rhetorical 'except/what else' introducing the limitation. چاره‌ای is an indefinite object meaning 'some remedy/solution'. The clause is elliptical: 'What can we do except [seek] some remedy?'.
segments.s025-g34: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('for/since/that'). بی‌چاره‌ای is used as 'a wretch/the helpless one' (indefinite noun). نیست is the negation 'is not / there is none'. پتیاره‌ای is an archaic/obscure noun (poetic) referring to a charm/talisman or miraculous cure; here negated to mean 'has no talisman/quick cure'.
segments.s025-g35: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed', a high-ranking military commander). گشاد here is poetic past of گشودن ('opened/revealed'); position after the subject is normal S–V. آن نهان = 'that hidden [thing/secret]' (demonstrative + adjective). از نهفت is an ablative phrase 'from hiding/from concealment' specifying source.
segments.s025-g36: همه خواب = 'the whole/dream' (here 'the dream' told to all). یک‌یک is an adverbial 'one by one / each in turn'. بدیشان = بد + ایشان, meaning 'to them'. بگفت is the past indicative 'he said / told' (archaic narrative past form). The clause means 'he told them the dream, one by one.'
segments.s025-g37: چنین 'thus' sets the manner. گفت here is the narrative past 'he said/spoke' (classical poetic usage). با + نامور ماهروی = 'to/with the famed, moon-faced [one]' — با marks the interlocutor/companion ('to the renowned, moon-faced person'). Word order places the manner and verb before the addressee.
segments.s025-g38: که introduces the quoted content/exhortation ('that / namely'). مگذار is a negative imperative 'do not allow' (2nd-person singular imperative). این را marks 'this' as the definite object. ره 'way' and چاره 'remedy' form the nominal phrase 'way of remedy'; جوی is the root/stem of جستن used as a verbal complement 'to seek' (i.e. 'to go seeking'). The clause therefore orders: 'Do not allow anyone to go seeking a way to remedy this.'
segments.s025-g39: نگین زمانه = 'the gem/jewel of the age' (a laudatory epithet). سر تخت = 'on the head/top of the throne' (placement phrase); تو است is the copula 'is yours / is you' — literal structure 'The jewel of the age is on the top of your throne.' Word order is emphatic: predicate before the possessor.
segments.s025-g40: جهان روشن = 'the world is bright/illuminated'. از + نامور بخت = 'because of / from your famed fortune' (از here gives the cause/agency). تو است is the copula 'it is your fortune' or more naturally 'the world shines by your famed fortune.'
segments.s025-g65: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'این راز با من' = 'this secret to/with me' (classical order: prepositional phrase before verb). 'بباید' is the modal 'must/ought' and takes the following past participle 'گشاد' (open) to mean 'must be opened/revealed'. The whole chunk means 'if this secret must be opened to me.'
segments.s025-g66: 'وگر' = 'and if / but if' introduces the alternative. 'سر به خواری' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'head into humiliation' (i.e. to abase oneself). Again 'بباید' = 'must', followed by the past form 'نهاد' (from نهادن, 'to place/put') used in the set phrase 'سر نهادن' = 'to bow/lay one's head'. Together: 'or if one must bow the head in humiliation.'
segments.s025-g67: 'لب موبدان' = 'the lips of the mobeds' (possessive/genitive). The adjectives follow the noun: 'خشک' = 'dry'. The comparative 'رخساره‌تر' is 'more (of) complexion' (رخساره = complexion/face; -تر = comparative), with the copula ('were') omitted as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s025-g68: 'زبان پر ز گفتار' = 'tongue full of speech' where 'پر' = 'full (of)' and the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'of/from' marks the content ('گفتار' = speech). 'با یکدگر' = 'with one another' (i.e. they spoke among themselves). Word order is typical poetic Persian: noun + adjective/phrase, then the companion phrase.
segments.s025-g69: 'که‌گر' = conditional 'if'. 'بودنی' = 'that-which-is' / 'existences' (abstract noun). 'باز' here functions as an adverb 'openly' (or 'again' in other contexts). 'گوییم' is 1st person plural present/subjunctive 'we say/speak' (subjunctive used in conditional contexts). 'راست' qualifies the utterance as 'true' or 'truthfully.' The phrase reads roughly 'if we speak openly of what exists, (it is) true.'
segments.s025-g70: This is a compressed predication: 'به جانست پیکار' = 'the battle is for life' or 'the struggle pertains to life' ('به' = 'for/to', 'جانست' here reads as 'is (of) life' or 'concerns life'). The coordinator 'و' = 'and' links the next clause: 'جان بی‌بهاست' = 'life is without price' (بی- = 'without', بها = 'value/price', -ست = 'is'). The line states that the fight concerns life and that life is invaluable/without price.
segments.s025-g71: 'وگر' = 'but if'. 'نشنود' is a negative present/subjunctive/3rd-person form 'does not hear' or 'will not listen'. 'بودنی‌ها' = 'the existents / those who are' (i.e. the living or those present). 'درست' = 'properly / rightly' modifies the verb. The clause says 'if the living do not hear properly.'
segments.s025-g72: 'بباید' = 'one must'. 'هم‌اکنون' = 'right now / immediately'. 'ز جان دست شست' is an idiom literally 'washed the hand from the life' (ز = 'from/of', جان = 'life/soul', دست شستن از = 'to wash one's hands of' = to renounce/abandon). Together: 'one must immediately renounce life' or 'one must at once wash one's hands of life.'
segments.s025-g17: یکی here is the indefinite 'someone' (not the numeral). بانگ بر زد is a light-verb construction: بانگ (a cry) is the object/thing produced and بر زد is the verb 'to utter/raise' (زد with the particle بر). به + خواب + اندرون places the action 'into/within the sleeping inner quarters' — به introduces the adverbial phrase 'into/as to' and اندرون specifies the inner part of the building.
segments.s025-g18: که introduces the result/clause 'that ...'. لرزان شد is a predicative phrase where لرزان (trembling) functions as an adjective and شد is the past copular/verb 'became/was' — together 'became trembling' = 'was shaken'. آن خانه صدستون is the subject noun phrase 'that house of a hundred columns' (آن = 'that/the').
segments.s025-g19: بجستند is the past plural of the verb 'to spring up' ('they sprang up'). خورشیدرویان is an epithet 'sun-faced (men)' serving as the subject. ز جای is a fixed prepositional phrase 'from (their) places', with ز (poetic variant of از) marking source.
segments.s025-g20: از آن غلغل نامور کدخدای is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from that renowned clamor of the kâdkhoday (headman)'; از introduces the cause/source, آن points back to 'that', غلغل نامور = 'renowned uproar/clamor', and کدخدای names whose clamor it was.
segments.s025-g21: چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); ضحاک را uses را as the indirect-object/target marker 'to Zahhak' (classical Persian uses را also to mark the person spoken to in some contexts), and ارنواز is the speaker's name in topic position.
segments.s025-g22: که introduces the quoted question. شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. چه بودت نگویی is an archaic ordering: چه = 'what', بودت = 'happened to you / was to you' (past-tense idea), نگویی here is the negative present/subjunctive form 'you do not say / will you not tell' — together 'why do you not tell what happened to you'. به راز = 'in secret' (به used adverbially + راز 'secret').
segments.s025-g23: که introduces the clause. خفته به آرام is a participial/adverbial phrase 'having slept calmly/peacefully' (خفته = 'asleep/having slept', به آرام = adverbial 'quietly/peacefully' formed with به). در خان خویش = 'in his/one's own khan/chamber' (در = 'in', خان here denotes a living-chamber/house-palace; خویش + ش together form the reflexive/possessive 'own/his').
segments.s025-g24: بر این‌سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner/thus' (بر here is the prefixed preposition used in set phrases). بترسیدی is the past second-person singular or narrative past 'you became afraid/you feared'. از جان خویش = 'for (or concerning) your own life' with از marking the source/concern and خویش + ش again indicating 'own/his'.
segments.s025-g73: This is a temporal-duration phrase with poetic word order: 'سه روز' (three days) gives the duration, 'اندر این کار' is a prepositional phrase 'in this matter', and 'شد روزگار' uses the past verb 'شد' with 'روزگار' to mean that that number of days came to pass (literary idiom: 'days happened/elapsed'). The verb precedes the noun 'روزگار' for emphasis, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s025-g74: 'سخن' is the object 'speech/words'. 'کس' here occurs in a negative/indefinite context meaning 'anyone/no one (who might speak)'. 'نیارست' is a negative/archaic verbal form meaning 'was not able/failed to manage' (sense not listed in the provided lemma senses, so given as -1). 'کرد آشکار' is a compound where 'کرد' (did/made) takes 'آشکار' ('clear') as its complement: 'made (it) clear' or 'succeeded in making (speech) plain' — here the negation attaches to the ability to make the speech clear.
segments.s025-g75: 'به‌روز چهارم' is an adverbial temporal expression 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز + ordinal). 'برآشفت' is the past-form of 'برآشفتن' meaning 'became angry/was enraged'. The verb appears before the noun 'شاه' (poetic inversion): 'the king became angry.'
segments.s025-g76: This phrase gives the recipient and agent of an implied sending: 'بر' = 'to/toward', 'آن موبدان' = 'those mōbeds/priests', and 'نماینده راه' = 'a messenger/representative (sent)'. The main sending verb is implied/elliptical in the line (a common poetic omission) — the noun phrase therefore functions as 'he sent a messenger to those mōbeds.' Word order places the indirect object before the messenger phrase for emphasis.
segments.s025-g77: 'که‌گر' = 'that if / if' introduces a conditional clause. 'زنده‌تان' is 'the living ones of yours' (زنده + possessive -تان) = 'if you keep them alive'. 'دار' in this phrasing functions as 'to keep/hold' in a terse/poetic use (the noun-form listed in the lemma set does not capture this verbal/lexical nuance, so sense is given as -1). 'باید' is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and 'بسود' 'to advantage' or 'for benefit' — together: 'if you keep them alive, it will be to your advantage.'
segments.s025-g78: 'و‌گر' = 'and if / but if' continues the conditional contrast. 'بودنی‌ها' is a plural formed from the adjective/participle 'بودنی' ('that which is to be / what must be') + plural suffix '-ها', i.e. 'the things destined/that ought to be done (or conversely, those meant to die/ be disposed of)'. 'بباید' is the modal 'must/ought' and 'نمود' (here used as an infinitive/nominalized verb) means 'to show/display' (poetic past-form used as infinitive). The clause means 'and if (it is) the destined/those matters, they must be shown/displayed.'
segments.s025-g79: 'همه موبدان' is the subject 'all the mōbeds/priests'. The following two words are coordinating adjectives: 'سرفگنده' (literally 'head-lowered') = 'abashed/with bowed heads' and 'نگون' = 'downcast/overthrown/ill‑fated'. The adjectives follow the noun, describing the priests' demeanour; this adjective stacking is typical in poetic description.
segments.s025-g80: 'پر از' = 'full of' (here 'پر' + preposition 'از'). 'هول دل' is a genitive-like noun cluster 'terror of the heart' = 'terror in their hearts'. The second half 'دیدگان پر ز خون' repeats the 'full of' construction with the poetic variant 'ز' for 'از' ('their eyes full of blood'). Note the parallel structure: inner state (terror in heart) then outward sign (eyes full of blood).
segments.s025-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a subordinate time-clause. The clause follows an SVO/agentless Persian order where the preposition از ('from/of') precedes روزگارش ('his time/life') and the quantity چهل سال ('forty years') is the thing that 'remained' (ماند) — i.e. 'there remained forty years of his life.' ماند is a past/perfect-type verb here meaning 'remained/was left.'
segments.s025-g2: نگر is an imperative/interjection 'look!' or 'behold' that launches the main clause. تا here functions as a complementizer meaning roughly 'so that/that' introducing what one should see. به + سر together mark direction/location 'to/upon the head'; the final -ش is a third-person possessive clitic ('his') attached awkwardly in the text (hence 'سرِش' = 'his head'). یزدان is the agent 'God', چه is the interrogative 'what', and راند is a past/fictive-past verb meaning 'drives/causes' (here 'would bring/bring upon'). The clause therefore asks the reader to 'see what God will bring upon his head.'
segments.s025-g3: در ایوان شاهی is a locative phrase 'in the royal iwan/portico' (در + noun + adjectival qualifier). شبی is an adverbial/time expression 'one night' or 'on a night', and دیر is the adjective/adverb 'late'. یاز is an older/poetic word here adjoined to دیر to mark the late-night setting; its exact nuance is archaic (see senses) but it functions adverbially with 'شبی دیر' to time the scene.
segments.s025-g4: به خواب اندرون بود is an existential/state clause: به خواب + بودن is an idiom meaning 'to be asleep'; اندرون 'inside/inner (quarters)' gives the location and بود is the past-tense copula 'was.' با with + a proper name (ارنواز) marks accompaniment: 'was inside asleep with Arnavaz.'
segments.s025-g5: چنان دید 'she saw thus/so' places the perception verb (دید) after the adverbial چنان. کز is the archaic fused form of 'که + از' functioning here as the conjunction 'that'/'from which' introducing the subordinate clause; کاخ شاهنشهان is a genitive noun phrase 'the palace of the Shahanshahs' (possession expressed by juxtaposition).
segments.s025-g6: سه جنگی is a numeral + noun phrase 'three warriors/fighters' (جنگی here adjectival/nominal meaning 'warlike/fighter'). پدید آمدی is a two-word verbal sequence meaning 'appeared/ came into being' (پدید 'appear', آمدی 'came' — the -ی ending is an older/poetic form of the verb); ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly.' The typical SOV order places the subject phrase first, then the verbal complex.
segments.s025-g7: دو مهتر 'two chiefs' is a simple numeral + noun. یکی کهتر اندر میان is an appositive/parenthetical: یکی 'one (of them)', کهتر 'the lesser/younger/inferior' describes that one, and اندر میان means 'in the midst' or 'among them.' The phrase sets up two persons and locates one of them as the smaller/younger standing in the middle.
segments.s025-g8: به بالای سرو و به فر کیان is a pair of prepositional/nominal phrases giving lofty attributes: به بالای سرو 'up/high above like a cypress' (به + بالای + سرو) uses بالای as 'above/atop' and سرو as a simile ('cypress' = tall/lofty). و links to the second phrase به فر کیان where فر means the royal radiance/glory and کیان is 'the kings' or 'the royal house' — together 'with the kings' royal radiance.'
segments.s025-g105: به بالا شود is an adverbial phrase (به + بالا) plus the verb شود 'becomes/ris[es]'; word order is subject-omitted poetic narration where the action is foregrounded. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and یکی سرو برز is the simile's vehicle: یکی 'one/a single' + سرو 'cypress' + برز 'high/exalted' (an adjective placed after the noun in this poetic phrase).
segments.s025-g106: به گردن is a directional/prepositional phrase 'onto (his) neck' (به = toward/onto + گردن = neck) functioning as the verb's object; برآرد is a third‑person singular classical present/relative form meaning 'he raises/lifts' (برآورد/برآرد). ز marks material 'of/from' so ز پولاد گرز means 'a mace of steel' (poetic order noun phrase: material before head noun).
segments.s025-g107: زند here is the action verb 'strikes/hits' (a classical form of زدن) — the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense, so it is marked separately. بر سرت is the prepositional phrase 'upon your head' (بر + سرت), and گرزه گاوسار is the subject phrase 'the mace of Gausar' in poetic apposition (owner/name follows the object without an izafe).
segments.s025-g108: بگیردت is a single verb form meaning 'seizes you' (بگیرد + enclitic ت = 'you'); زار modifies the result (here 'sorely' or 'afflicted/wretched'), and the clause continues with و 'and' plus ببنددت, another verb with enclitic ت meaning 'binds you' (here 'bind/restrain'). خوار is a predicative/adjectival result 'in disgrace/despised'.
segments.s025-g109: بدو is a dative 'to him' (מדיע/to him), and گفت here functions as 'said' (the provided senses did not include the speech-verb sense). ضحاک ناپاک دین is an epithet: ضحاک the proper name, ناپاک 'impure/impious' modifying دین 'religion' used idiomatically to label him as 'impious' or 'of impure faith'.
segments.s025-g110: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. بنددم is a first‑person verbal form meaning 'should I bind / do I bind' (classical conditional/intentional sense), از منش is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from (his) disposition/character' (از = from/because of + منش = character), and چیست کین is a question 'what is this kīn (hatred/grudge)?' with ست (is) linking چی (what) to کین (grudge/hatred).
segments.s025-g111: دلاور is the agent 'the hero/valiant one'. بدو is the dative 'to him'. گفت‌گر here should be read as two pieces (گفت + گر): گفت 'said' followed by the conditional particle گر 'if'; the token list's senses do not reflect that split. بخردی is the conditional/predicative formation 'if you are wise' (ب- prefix + خرد root + -ی second‑person ending) rather than the nominal sense given in the list.
segments.s025-g112: کسی here functions as an indefinite negative 'no one' in this proverb-like statement. بی‌بهانه is an adverb/adjective 'without cause/unprovoked', نسازد is the present/classical form 'makes/does' (used here to state a general truth: 'does/makes'), and بدی is the noun 'evil/harm' — together: 'no one makes evil without cause.'
segments.s025-g97: کجا here opens the clause and functions idiomatically as a discourse marker introducing the statement; the substantive phrase 'نام او' (his name) precedes the predicate noun 'آفریدون' and the past copula 'بود' comes at the end, so the line reads literally 'his name Afrīdūn was.' The verb بود is the past linking verb 'was.'
segments.s025-g98: زمین را uses را as a topical/definite marker putting 'the earth' into focus; 'سپهری همایون' is an adjectival/epithet phrase ('heavenly, auspicious') that serves as the predicate describing the earth's fortune, with بود again the past copula at the end.
segments.s025-g99: هنوز at the clause start means 'still/yet' and scopes over the whole clause; 'آن سپهبد' is the definite noun phrase 'that spāhbed'; 'ز مادر نزاد' is a genitival/origination phrase 'born of (his) mother' with نزاد functioning as the past/participial sense 'born/produced,' forming a nominal clause whose main verbal sense completes in the next segment.
segments.s025-g100: نیامد is the negative past 'did not come' and here serves as the main verb that negates the previous born-clause ('had not yet been born'); گه means 'occasion/time' and governs the coordinated nouns 'پرسش و سرد باد' ('a time for questioning and a cold wind'). باد is the noun 'wind' (not the optative particle) so the whole phrase says such an occasion or even a cold wind had not yet arrived.
segments.s025-g101: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when/after'; او is the subject and زاید is the verb 'is born/comes into being' (classical present/past sense), with از مادر marking origin. پرهنر is a postpositive adjective 'full of skill/talented' modifying the subject 'he.'
segments.s025-g102: به سان is a simile marker 'in the manner of/like'; درختی is the simile noun 'a tree' (indefinite with the -ی suffix); شود is the present/future copulative 'becomes' and بارور is the predicate adjective 'fruitful/fertile' — together 'becomes fruitful like a tree.'
segments.s025-g103: به مردی رسد is a fixed idiom 'he reaches manhood' where به + noun + رسد express attainment; بر + کشد here forms the prefixed verb برکشـد 'raises up' (بر as an intensifying/prefix), with سر the object 'his head' and به ماه a directional phrase 'toward the moon' (poetic image of lifting one's head). Note کشد is the poetic/older form of کشیدن 'to pull/raise.'
segments.s025-g104: کمر جوید is an idiomatic clause: جوید ('seeks') takes کمر ('girdle/waist') and here conveys girding one's loins / preparing oneself; the coordinating conjunction و links the subsequent list of sought items — تاج و تخت و کلاه — which are all direct objects of جوید ('seeks/desires crown, throne, and cap').
segments.s025-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دانسته شد is a passive/perfect construction ('was/ became known') — دانسته (known) + شد (become/was). چاره‌ساز آن زمان is a noun phrase 'the remedy-maker / solver of that time' (چاره‌ساز = 'one who makes remedies/solves problems', آن زمان = 'that time'), functioning as the patient/subject of دانسته شد; the copula/auxiliary is already expressed by شد.
segments.s025-g50: به خیره is an adverbial phrase 'in astonishment / with eyes fixed' (به used as an adverbial marker). مترس is a negative imperative 'fear not / be not afraid'. از introduces the object of fear ('from / of'), and بد بدگمان is a doubled descriptive phrase meaning 'the evil of ill‑opinion / ill‑suspicions' (بد intensifies بدگمان). The whole phrase modifies the imperative: 'Do not, in astonishment, fear the harm of ill suspicions.'
segments.s025-g51: This clause is structured as 'X را خوش‌آمد Y' — here شه پر منش را (the king of noble character, marked by را) is the dative/experiencer and سخن is the thing that pleases; خوش‌آمد is the predicative 'was pleasing / pleased'. In other words, 'the speech was pleasing to the king of noble lineage.' Note پر + منش is a compound describing the king ('پر' = 'full of', 'منش' = 'disposition/origin/character').
segments.s025-g52: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for / because'). آن سرو سیمین is a poetic epithet 'that silvery cypress' (سرو a cypress = tall/beautiful person), and برافگند بن is a verbal phrase where برافگند (threw off/uprooted) takes بن ('root') as its object; word order places the verb before the object in this classical style. The clause gives the reason for the king's pleasure: 'for that (noble one) uprooted the root.'
segments.s025-g53: This descriptive clause omits an explicit copula (typical in classical Persian) and reads 'جهان از شب تیره چون پر زاغ' = 'the world, from the dark night, like the wing of a crow' — i.e. 'the world was dark as a raven's wing.' از marks source/state ('from night'), تیره is the adjective 'dark', and چون introduces the simile comparing the darkness to a crow's wing.
segments.s025-g54: همانگه = 'at that moment'. The clause سر از کوه بر زد چراغ literally 'the lamp struck/raised its head from the mountain' — a poetic way to say 'a lamp/ light appeared/rose from the mountain.' Note the verb complex بر زد (بر + زد) conveys upward/appearing motion and the subject چراغ comes at the end (verb before subject is common in classical Persian).
segments.s025-g55: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said'; که introduces the reported content ('that'). بر گنبد لاژورد is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon the lapis (lažvard) dome' (بر = 'on/upon', گنبد = 'dome', لاژورد = the lapis-lazuli/azure material), marking where the following action is to take place.
segments.s025-g56: بگسترد is the classical verbal form 'would spread / spread' (ب- prefix plus present/subjunctive stem), here reporting the predicted/imagined action. خورشید is the subject 'the sun', and یاقوت زرد is a compound epithet 'yellow ruby' (یاقوت used poetically of gemlike color; زرد 'yellow') modifying the sun. The verb precedes its subject and modifiers in this inverted, poetic ordering.
segments.s025-g9: کمر بستن is a light verb construction meaning “to gird the waist / to gird oneself” (کمر = waist; بستن here = bind/gird). The conjunction و links that action to رفتن; رفتن is an omitted-subject past action here (Epic Persian often omits the subject) rendered as “(he) went/set off.” شاهوار is an adjectival/adverbial qualifier meaning “regal / stately” modifying رفتن and placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s025-g10: به چنگ is an adverbial phrase meaning “in (his) hand / holding” where به is used in the instrumental/locative sense (“by / in”) and چنگ here denotes a grip/handlike hold. اندرون گرزه گاوسار is a noun phrase: اندرون qualifies گرزه as “the inner/inside mace,” and گاوسار is a following name/apposition identifying that mace. The whole phrase describes him going while clutching the inner mace called Gausar.
segments.s025-g11: This is a compressed scene-setting clause. دمان (literally “hem / skirt”) is used idiomatically with پیش ضحاک to mean “to the skirts/threshold (i.e. up to the presence) of Zahhak.” رفتی is 2nd-person past “you went/rushed,” and به جنگ is the prepositional phrase “into battle/into the fight.” Word order places the locative/figurative object (دمان) and the prepositional phrase (پیش ضحاک) before the verb, which is typical in classical narrative.
segments.s025-g12: نهادی here functions as the past of نهادن (“you placed/put”); the provided lemma senses do not include this verbal sense, so the exact listed sense is not applicable. به گردن is a locative/goal phrase “onto the neck,” and پالهنگ is the object placed there (a palahang, i.e. leopard or leopard‑skin). برش in the string as printed does not correspond to the sense ‘cut’ from the provided list in this context; here it reads as a clitic/relational element meaning roughly “on/for him” (but that grammatical use is not among the given senses).
segments.s025-g13: همی is the old narrative/progressive particle meaning “kept on / continually,” and تاختی is the 2nd-person past “you charged / you galloped.” تا is the preposition “until / as far as,” leading to دماوند کوه, a common poetic order where the specific name precedes the generic noun: “Mount Damavand.” Together they mean “you kept charging as far as Mount Damavand.”
segments.s025-g14: کشان and دوان are two participial/verbal-stem forms joined by و describing simultaneous manner: “dragging and running / dragging and pursuing.” از پس is an adverbial phrase “from behind / in pursuit,” and اندر گروه places the action “within/among the company (troop).” The clause therefore describes manner and direction of movement (they dragged and ran after from behind among the troop).
segments.s025-g15: بپیچید is the past (3rd singular) of پیچیدن with the prefix ب-, here “he turned / twisted.” The subject is named immediately after: ضحاک بیدادگر, “Zahhak the oppressor/tyrant,” a noun plus epithet in apposition. The verb–subject order (verb before subject) is common in epic lines.
segments.s025-g16: بدریدش is the past verb بدریدن “he tore / he ripped” with the enclitic ش = “him/it” (object). از هول is a causal phrase “from terror / through fright.” گفتی here is difficult: the listed senses are about speech/dialogue and do not match the apparent poetic usage; in context it functions parenthetically (often in classical lines words like گفتی can mean “it is said” or introduce a reported remark) or may be an adverbial intensifier — the exact classical sense is uncertain here. جگر is “heart / seat of feeling” and is the affected organ in the violent image. Overall: “he tore him in terror — his heart (as the line relates).”
segments.s025-g81: 'از' + 'آن نامداران' marks constituency: 'from/among those renowned (ones)'. 'بسیار' here quantifies/strengthens 'هوش' (i.e. 'much intelligence' or 'very intelligent'). Word order is the typical Persian prepositional phrase: preposition 'از' + determiner 'آن' + plural noun 'نامداران' followed by modifier 'بسیار' and head noun 'هوش'.
segments.s025-g82: 'یکی بود' is the simple past/existential 'there was one' (یکی = 'one'). The predicate is a nominal phrase 'بینادل و تیزگوش' joined by 'و' ('and'). 'بود' is past of 'to be'. Note the adjective phrase follows the copula as in Persian.
segments.s025-g83: A string of adjectival predicates joined by 'و' ('and'): 'خردمند' (wise), 'بیدار' (awake/alert), 'زیرک' (clever/shrewd). 'به نام' is the idiom 'by the name (called)', with 'به' functioning as the prepositional marker linking to the following name (the actual name may be supplied or implied).
segments.s025-g84: 'کز' begins a relative/qualifying clause ('who/of whom' or 'from whom'). 'آن موبدان' = 'those mobeds/priests' is the group being referred to; 'او' is the pronoun 'he'. The verb 'زدی' (from زدَن) is used here in the past to mean 'uttered/struck/sounded' — in this context best rendered 'spoke' or 'made himself heard' — and 'پیش‌گام' is the predicate noun 'foremost/first mover'. The clause thus means 'who among those mobeds he was the first (to speak/step forward)'; note Persian places the verb toward the end of the relative clause.
segments.s025-g85: 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessor -ش). 'تنگ‌تر گشت' is comparative 'became tighter/more constricted' with 'گشت' (past of گشتن) functioning as 'became/turned'. The coordinating 'و' links a second resultant state 'ناباک شد' where 'شد' is another past/copulative 'became' and 'ناباک' describes the new moral/attitudinal state (here contextually 'unashamed/bold'). The two predicates form a sequence of internal change.
segments.s025-g86: 'گشاده‌زبان' is a compound adjective 'open‑tongued' = 'outspoken'. 'پیش' with a person ('پیش ضحاک') means 'in the presence of' or 'before' (i.e. spoken to/at Zahhak). 'شد' again is 'became'; together 'گشاده‌زبان پیش ضحاک شد' = 'he became outspoken before Zahhak'.
segments.s025-g87: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' where 'بدو' = 'to him' (prepositional/indirect object) and 'گفت' is the past 'said'. The quoted imperative follows: 'پردخته کن سر ز باد' — 'پردخته کن' is an imperative 'make/perform in a quick/thorough manner' (پردخته here = 'well‑executed/quickly done') + 'کن' (imperative 'do'); 'سر' = 'head' (direct object). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' and 'باد' in such older usage can function as an optative/instantaneous sense ('at once' / 'let it be'). So the phrase issues the command 'cut off his head at once.'
segments.s025-g88: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because'. 'جز مرگ را کس ز مادر نزاد' is a negative‑exclusive construction: 'جز ... کس ... نزاد' = 'no one except ... was born'. 'جز مرگ' = 'except Death'; 'را' is the definite/object marker attached to 'مرگ' for emphasis; 'کس' = 'anyone/no one' (here meaning 'anyone'); 'ز مادر' = 'from a mother' (i.e. 'born of a mother'); 'نزاد' (from زادَن) = 'was born/comes into being' (archaic form). The whole clause: 'for no one is born of a mother except Death.'
segments.s025-g113: برآید is a poetic/present-form verb meaning “comes/comes about” (verb first here). به دست تو is a prepositional phrase “into your hands / in your power” (دست here reading as ‘power/control’); هوش پدرش is the thing that comes (هوش = intellect/wits) with the possessive suffix ش attached to پدر marking “his father’s.” Word order: Verb → locative phrase → noun phrase (possessor marked by ش).
segments.s025-g114: از آن درد is a causal/ablative phrase “from/ because of that pain.” گردد is the present/optative form “becomes.” پر از کینه is the idiom “full of hatred” where پر takes از to introduce the content (از = ‘of’ here). سرش ‘his head’ uses the suffix ش to mark possession; overall: “because of that pain his head becomes full of hatred.”
segments.s025-g115: یکی is an indefinite ‘a certain.’ گاو برمایه is a noun phrase ‘a valuable / capital cow’ (برمایه = property/capital). خواهد is the modal/future marker ‘will / wants to’; بدن here appears as a verb meaning ‘to give’ in the poetic line, but that verbal sense is not listed in the provided senses (see note). The subject is indefinite/omitted — typical in Persian.
segments.s025-g116: جهانجوی را is a definite object marked by را (‘the world-seeker / ambitious man’). دایه is the noun ‘nurse, wet‑nurse.’ خواهد + بدن again expresses future/giving (‘will give [a nurse]’), with بدن functioning here as the verb ‘to give’ in the poetic usage (this verbal sense is not among the listed senses). Word order: object (with را) precedes the predicate.
segments.s025-g117: تبه گردد is a poetic phrase ‘becomes ruined / goes awry’ (تبه here = ruin/mischief). آن هم means ‘that too / even that.’ به دست تو is the agentive/ablative phrase ‘by your hand / in your power.’ بر is a preposition here meaning ‘against’ and links to the following clause. Word order: predicate → topical element → agent phrase → preposition introducing next clause.
segments.s025-g118: بدین is the archaic/poetic compound meaning ‘with this / at this’ (بـ + این). کین = ‘grudge / vengeance.’ کشد is the verb ‘strikes / draws / wields’ in context (here used transitively); that verbal sense is not among the provided senses for کشد in the list, so it is marked unavailable. گرزه گاوسر = ‘the mace Gausar’ (گرزه ‘mace’ with گاوسر a proper name/epithet). The sequence is typical V + object (poetic inversion possible).
segments.s025-g119: چو بشنید is a temporal clause ‘when he heard’ (چو = when). ضحاک is the subject ‘Zahhāk.’ بگشاد گوش literally ‘opened (his) ear’ — a poetic idiom meaning he pricked up his ears / gave attention; بگشاد is an older/poetic verb form ‘opened.’ Word order: temporal marker → verb → subject → verb phrase.
segments.s025-g120: ز تخت اندر افتاد is a compact poetic clause ‘he fell from the throne’ (ز = from; اندر = into/within used poetically; افتاد = fell). و زو رفت هوش continues ‘and from it/there his awareness went’ (زو = from it; رفت here = ‘left/vanished’ and هوش = ‘awareness / sense’). The construction هوش رفت is an idiom meaning ‘lost consciousness’ or ‘his wits left him.’
segments.s025-g57: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a title). به هر جا که is a locative phrase 'to/at every place that / wherever' (به + noun + relative که). بد موبدی is a noun phrase 'an evil/bad mobad' with the adjective بد preceding and modifying موبدی (mobad = Zoroastrian priest). Together the segment sets the location/condition for what the spahbed did.
segments.s025-g58: This is a coordinated nominal phrase giving what the spahbed obtained/assigned: سخن‌دان 'an eloquent/speaking person' and بیداردل 'a wakeful/tender‑hearted (person)'. بخردی here names the quality or office related to wisdom/prudence (a noun, 'wisdom' or 'a wise one'); the whole phrase reads as the people/qualities the spahbed provided where bad mobads were. Word order is noun + و + noun + noun (the last acting as a qualifying noun).
segments.s025-g59: ز کشور 'from the country/realm' is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = from). به نزدیک خویش is 'to near his own (person)' — خویش is formed from خوی + ش (kinship/own + his) meaning 'his own' or 'to himself'. آورید is a classical past/perfective form of آوردن 'to bring' (here translated 'brought' or 'caused to be brought'); in older narrative style the form appears as آورید.
segments.s025-g60: بگفت is an old narrative perfect/past 'he said / he told'. آن جگرخسته خوابی is 'that dream of the heart‑weary (man)' — جگرخسته 'heart‑weary' modifies خوابی. خوابی is the noun 'dream' here (not 'sleep'); the relative clause که دید 'that (he) saw' modifies خوابی, so the whole means 'the dream that the heart‑weary man saw.'
segments.s025-g61: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly / in private' modifying the verbal action. سخن کردشان is the light‑verb construction: سخن (speech) + کرد + شان (enclitic 'them') — literally 'did speech to them' = 'spoke to them'. آشکار here means 'clear' or 'made manifest' and functions as the complement/result of his speaking ('spoke secretly and laid things open/clear').
segments.s025-g62: ز نیک و بد و گردش روزگار is a list of the topics he revealed: ز (about/from) + نیک و بد 'the good and the bad', و گردش روزگار 'and the turning / course (گردش) of fate (روزگار)'. گردش روزگار is an idiomatic pair meaning the 'turns of fate' or 'course of fortune'.
segments.s025-g63: که introduces an interrogative/exclamatory clause here 'when / that'. بر من زمانه کی آید بسر literally 'when will the time/fortune come upon me to pass' — بر (upon) + من (me) + زمانه (time/fortune) and کی آید (when will come) with بسر 'to pass/happen' (classical phrase meaning 'when will my time/fortune arrive'). کی is the interrogative 'when' and آید is the verb 'comes'.
segments.s025-g64: که را باشد این تاج و تخت و کمر asks 'to whom will this (be)?' که را is an interrogative 'who (as object)' with را as the object/definiteness marker; باشد is the present/subjunctive form 'be / will be' used optatively/indirectly here; این تاج و تخت و کمر lists the possessions 'this crown, throne and girdle' (کمر = girdle/belt, a symbolic insignia). The clause asks who will hold those honors.

sentences.s025-s45: There were many sovereigns before you who were worthy of a moon-like throne.
sentences.s025-s46: He numbered abundant sorrows and joys, then departed and entrusted the world to another.
sentences.s025-s47: If an iron burden should alight at the foot of your heaven, you would not remain in your place.
sentences.s025-s48: There will be someone who afterward will cast your throne to the earth and bring your head and fortune low.
sentences.s025-s21: You have the world beneath your ring — enemies, people, birds, demons, and fairies.
sentences.s025-s22: From every country gather together the chiefs — the astrologers and the enchanters.
sentences.s025-s23: Speak plainly all that the mobeds (priests) say; investigate and search out the truth.
sentences.s025-s24: Take heed: whose hand holds your intelligence? Count whether it is from among men, or from demons and fairies.
sentences.s025-s13: The land of seven realms is under your command; foes, snares, and the people are bound by your covenant.
